title: Smudge
body:
Some days,
I feel like a smudge
on a page too white.
Like no matter how sharp my edges are,
the page keeps trying to clean me off.

I walk through halls
where my hoodie gets more attention
than my abilities.
Where my silence feels too loud,
and my voice —
too much.

I sit in classrooms named after men
who never meant for me to be here.
Learning about "diversity"
from PowerPoints that treat me like proof
that the system's working.

They smile at me like I'm a checkbox
come to life.

And sometimes,
I swear I feel like a zoo animal.
Eyes on me when I speak,
like I'm performing —
like they're waiting to see
if I'll roar or behave.

Their smiles tight,
their questions polite,
but the air thick
with curiosity
and quiet fear.

You can feel it —
the smug looks,
the subtle ones,
the ones that say you're lucky to be here
without saying it out loud.

And I laugh sometimes,
louder than I mean to,
just to break the pressure.

But still,
I stay.

I walk through that campus
like it owes me something —
because it does.

I raise my hand
because my existence is an answer
before I even speak.

And when the night comes
and the hills empty out,
the wind talks to me soft —
keep going, young man.

So I do.

Because being here
ain't just about grades.
It's about grounding.
It's about every Black kid
who'll come after me
and feel the ground shake less
because I was here first.

I'm tired, yeah.
But I'm standing.
And I'm staying.

Because we been caged,
we been stared at,
we been talked over —
and still,
we roar.
